Opinion Piece

In last week’s version we discussed Trump’s BBB and some market implications that went along with it. This week, the new tax and spending bill has passed both the Senate and House, and is set to be signed July 4th. If you missed last week's version I copied it down below:

“(Week 6/23-27) Big Beautiful Bill: Trump's new tax bill passes the Senate, marking the largest non-discretionary spending cut in U.S. history. The bill is designed to stabilize spending and up the rate of growth to stabilize the deficit to GDP ratio (currently 6.4%). Despite this growth structure, the bill’s tax cuts are projected to add over $3.3 trillion to the deficit, pushing U.S. debt past $40 trillion in the next decade. This move has already influenced the bond market, where markets are bracing for more Treasury issuance and higher yields for bonds with longer maturities. This bill may boost economic growth and business confidence into 2026, but the yield curve is expected to steepen and upward pressure on long term rates could continue.”

As the new bill has now passed both the House and Senate we may begin to see more adverse effects. To start, the bill proposes increased tax cuts for big businesses and incentives for small businesses, as well as more infrastructure and defense spending. We have already seen growth in the market, driven from gains across the board, with both the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq Composite hitting new all time highs. Although the new bill isn’t the only catalyst for growth this week, I am sure we will begin to see more price movement shortly. I think it is also important to note that despite the Fed and Chair Powell’s calls for increased inflation in coming months, we are currently holding in a potential soft landing from tariff and trade blowback. While President Trump is screaming for rate cuts, this week's data has reaffirmed the Fed’s wait and see policy as more data arises. Without diving too deep into the nuance of the bill, the underlying structure represents opportunities for more spending and more liquidity into the market → pushing equity prices up. While tax cuts equate to higher profit margins and therefore better earning results, company shares will likely continue to rise.

The problem arises here, as I mentioned in last week's brief. With the deficit widening from tax cuts and increased spending, a surge in Treasury issuance may steepen the yield curve and increase rates on bonds such as the 10 or 30 year treasury note. This week's conflicting labor data and the expectations for no cut in July have already risen the 10-year up almost 6 basis points this week to 4.34%. Last year in September, the 10-year was yielding 3.6%. As spending increases simultaneously with the yield curve, this could pressure tech and growth stocks. Bonds are referred to as a safer asset, but if the return is perceived as higher/safer than riskier stock names, then a lot of the capital going into these growth companies could be funneled into treasury yields. Also, if data comes out that inflation is rising and everyday consumers are hurting, the long awaited rate cut could be stalled towards year end further hurting the market. Altogether this results in a potential market pullback.

Another factor is this idea that the market is currently being driven by FOMO (fear of missing out) and is currently overvalued. This thesis rests on the fact that investors are buying back into the market to avoid missing out on gains from the April dip, as well as short squeezing, where investors who are hedging against downside risk need to cover their short positions by buying back into the market. We saw this happen in 2020 after covid, where all this “forced” buying led to a sharp market pullback. Right now, using the S&P 500 as our benchmark, the loss from April tariffs has been fully recovered and the index is hitting new highs. But, the average stock within the index has not performed so well. In the S&P 500 alone, gains have been majority driven by four major companies: Nvidia, Netflix, Microsoft, and Meta. Just these four companies alone make up 20% of the index weight. While these few companies driving gains is not obviously a negative for the index, it does indicate that a correction may be needed to digest gains and let other companies catch up before resuming its current bullish trend.

Lastly, we can look through a more technical lense, like a sigma chart of some of our current indexes, and compare with the CNN Fear and Greed Index. The F&G Index measures investor sentiment and market mood trends, while a sigma chart looks at standard deviation from the mean, giving us insight into unusual trends. Firstly, while looking at the Fear and Greed Index, we see it lies at extreme greed. Specifically, the Put and Call Options part of this index being at extreme greed should serve as a warning that we may be reaching a point of extremely over bullish sentiment. When too many market participants are on the same side of the trade, the risk of negative factors can trigger rapid unwinding and profit-taking, causing a sharp market pullback. Now when looking at the sigma chart of the S&P 500 and DJIA, we also see levels on the upper bounds of their sigma bands, with the DJIA actually currently entering even higher levels. This positioning reflects extreme bullish momentum with unsupported fundamentals, causing overvaluation or the potential for a market pullback.

So, to answer my question from before; is the market in trouble? My conclusion lies in between two points:

  1. Yes, the market appears to be close to a correction. With our current overly bullish sentiment we are overvaluing the market and driving unsupported valuations. Driven largely by hype around AI and tech growth stocks, while broader market participation remains weak, the new legislation could end up having adverse effects on yields and the current allocation into equities. Secondly, all the data I just covered points to a potential pullback of some sort, where the indexes correct to reset expectations and reestablish a healthier foundation before resuming any sustained bullish trend.
  2. Does the market care? Do investors? We all know about the argument for rate cuts. Although President Trump brands Fed Chair Powell as “Too Late Powell” as rates still stay elevated, the current Chair is adamant that inflation will come. Despite outward backlash forefronted by the President, the Fed is sticking with a “wait and see policy.” Looking ahead though, Chair Jerome Powell is expected to step aside when his current term ends, opening the door for a new appointee under the current administration. When President Trump appoints the next Fed Chair, markets can be all but certain a shift in monetary policy will happen: policy aimed towards easing and interest rate cuts to support growth. Some investors know this, and the question becomes, do they care what Powell says? If they know rate cuts are coming, isn’t it better to enter the market now, or even better to have entered after the April dip in expectations of tariff related inflation and rate cuts? This way of thinking could end up prolonging bullish sentiment and driving current highs even higher. As investors know rate cuts lie in the not too distant future, despite signs pointing to a correction and overhyped rallies, the market may choose to ignore key signals and continue to rise.

Sources: Google Finance, Market Watch, CME Fedwatch, Yahoo Finance, Reuters, New York Times, Bloomberg, Wall St Journal, Washington Post, US Department of the Treasury, CNBC, BravosResearch

Noah Halfon
